description: Analyzing whether a sudden drop in user retention was caused by a UI change or a pricing change. Competing hypotheses.

chat.content: We had a 14% drop in week-4 retention starting on March 11. Two things shipped that week: a redesigned onboarding flow on March 9, and a pricing-page A/B test that ran from March 10 to March 24. We need to figure out which one caused the drop, if either.
Do we have a control group for either change?
Only for pricing. The pricing A/B was 50/50 random assignment. Onboarding was rolled out to 100%, no control.
Then the cleanest signal is whether the pricing test arm shows the drop and the control arm doesn't. Have we looked?
We pulled the numbers. Pricing test arm: 13.8% drop. Pricing control arm: 13.1% drop. The drop is in both arms. So the pricing change is not the dominant cause.
That's pretty conclusive on pricing. The remaining candidate is onboarding, but we have no control. Can we use a pre-post comparison with a control series?

We have organic-search-only signups as a quasi-control because they go through a different onboarding (legacy). Their week-4 retention is flat over the same window. That's suggestive but not airtight: organic-search users may differ in baseline.
It's a weak control, but better than nothing. If organic is flat and paid-funnel users (who got the new onboarding) dropped, that's consistent with onboarding being the cause.
Right. So our working hypothesis is: the redesigned onboarding caused the drop, not pricing. Pricing is ruled out by the A/B.
We should note an open question: were there any other infra or product changes in that week that we haven't accounted for? A confounding ship would invalidate the conclusion.
Good catch. I'll check the changelog for that week. Open question for now.

Also — we should NOT roll back the onboarding change yet. Even if it's correlated, we don't know if it's the activation funnel or the post-onboarding flow that dropped users. Let's instrument first, decide later.
Agreed. So the plan is: keep onboarding live, instrument the funnel, and reassess in two weeks?
Yes. Two weeks of instrumentation, then we look again. And we explicitly do not claim onboarding causes the drop in any external comms until we have funnel data.
What about the question of seasonality? Could March 11 just be a calendar effect — spring break, end of fiscal year?

Possible. Last year's same week didn't show this drop, but we shipped many other things since then so the year-over-year comparison is contaminated. I'd treat seasonality as a tentative rival hypothesis we can't fully exclude with current data.
Recorded as a tentative rival, not ruled out.
Last thing: there's a genuine tension here. The PM wants a fast answer for next week's review. The analysis discipline says wait for funnel data. We can't both move fast and be rigorous.
Real conflict. We need to surface that to leadership, not paper over it.
